result:
The first time I heard the word *sonder* I was sitting in an airport bar, nursing a ginger ale and pretending to read while secretly eavesdropping on the couple to my left. Sonder, the narrator of the podcast murmured, is the moment you realize that every random passer-by is living a life as vivid and tangled and heartbreaking as your own. It’s a single syllable that tastes like metal under the tongue—the shock of disorientation when the human anthology you’ve been skimming abruptly reveals that every chapter has the same emotional page count as yours.  

There is a geometry to airports that encourages this particular hallucination of scale. Twelve gates to my right, a woman in cat-eye sunglasses was FaceTiming someone in Bantu; in the Hudson News by Gate A7, two teenagers flirted over matching copies of *The Anthropocene Reviewed*. Nobody was showing their grief, the way you fold the worst pages of a book inward to spare strangers the sight of the violence. Everyone was in transit, occupying the rare interstitial minute where no one expects you to be who you are at home. I have never seen so many parallel lives overlap without touching.

Sometimes I’ll pretend I’m equipped with some gentle form of X-ray vision that can reveal, suspended above every head like minimalist air-traffic control, the three most urgent things that person is carrying right now. The mother herding twin boys into the restroom stall: chemo follow-up, dissertation on hold, and a wedding dress in the garment bag dragging behind her like a silver fishtail. The teenager scrolling TikTok with Bose headphones: secret poetry blog, estranged father in prison, and the quiz tomorrow on *Othello*. I know I’m largely wrong; my guesses are ragged confetti shot at a piñata from a great distance. But the exercise steadies me. It reminds me that every “random person” my life glances off is actually a universe, coiled and trembling like a nebula in a mason jar.

I think that is why I became a cantor, of all things—an unlikely profession for an atheist who faints at the sight of needles. My grandfather was the kind of devout whose voice lifted like orchard heat over Friday night tables. When he was dying, throat ragged from the intubation, he made me promise to keep the minor-key songs alive. It was a practical request: someone in the family should be able to sing *Ein Keloheinu* without cracking. I attended to it the way other people attend to property transfers, a bequest of harmony rather than capital. On the first anniversary of his death I stood at the back of the synagogue and opened my mouth, and instead of grief the sound coming out felt like fruit splitting open. The congregants turned, half startled. The rabbi let me finish. When the last note settled, the woman second row from the back was weening silently over her siddur. I had never seen her before. But her shoulders shook with the same quake lines that had fractured my ribs for months, and in that moment the boundary between her sorrow and mine evaporated, the way two puddles essentially become one rain.

Years later, I sang *Kol Nidre* annually for a dwindling congregation in Brooklyn. The acoustics were awful—you had to lean into the room’s reluctance like pushing a piano uphill—but once a year the sanctuary became a vacuum chamber big enough to hold every regret we could fit into its four walls. On Yom Kippur eve we sang the melody as a question whose answer is mercy. And each time, somewhere in the second repetition, I’d glance sideways at Mr. Feldsher, ninety-two years old, who had once been a partisan blowing up Nazi supply trains under moonless skies. During the line *“May we be absolved of vows that do not fulfill themselves,”* his eyes always went glassy. I never asked him which vow was dissolving in the music; it seemed crass to demand specifics from a private exorcism. But we sang it together, me anchor and him ballast, and between us the air shimmered like asphalt in July.

Grief, I’ve found, has a fractal shape. What you think is a single, sharp object turns out, on closer inspection, to be replicating fern leaves made of grief, the veins of which are smaller leaves made of still smaller griefs. The afternoon I miscarried at fifteen weeks I was actually humming the baby’s name—Ayla, light—while crushing tomatoes for soup. There was no Hollywood moment of breaking dishes. When the bleeding started it was merely a fact, a shift in the ledger. Yet every time I slice an overripe tomato now, the dark gel of the seeds feels like the spill I tried to hide under the rug. This is how people carry invisible reliquaries: an aisle seat, the word *sonder*, the smell of crushed basil diluted with hospital lemon soap. We are each an accidental museum of small, obsolete violences.

An acquaintance told me once that anxiety is the tax we pay for imagination. I thought of that the day I sat behind a woman on the subway who was chewing an entire pack of bubblegum at once—twelve grape rectangles trampling each other like purple livestock. The train stalled between stations. Phones lost service. Eight minutes became eighteen. Instead of panic, a quiet theater spontaneously erupted. The man opposite began juggling phantom scarves. The woman opposite translated her elbow crease into the outline of the Appalachian Trail. A little girl taught us all how to disconnect the emergency intercom and turn it into a kazoo. Nobody asked what the others did for a living or what they believed about reality; we were simply the sudden fellowship of horizontal time. When the lights flickered back on, applause bubbled up without committee vote, the way steam lifts from wet laundry in winter.

Last spring I flew to Lisbon to scatter what was left of my grandfather next to Atlantic waves the color of dishwater steel. The customs agent glanced at my paperwork, then at the urn wrapped in teal velvet inside my carry-on. I braced for challenge, but instead he produced a tiny laminated card—photo of his own grandfather fishing, a sepia sunset behind him. “Welcome home,” he said. He had no right to say that to me, a secular wanderer holding ash and mp3 files of synagogue songs. Yet the sentence landed softly, a passport stamp on skin. That night I stood thigh-deep in Portuguese water so cold my ankles went pinprick white. I released a swirl that immediately blurred into foam and was gone, so fast I wondered if I had imagined everything. Then a lump lodged in my throat: the same pitch in *El Mol* where the cantor’s falsetto breaks on the Hebrew word for return. I sang the melody. A surfer flicking past mimicked the rhythm on his board, off-key but earnest, shouting *obrigado* over the wind. Meanwhile, back in Brooklyn, Mr. Feldsher was probably fixing tea, unaware he was the counterweight in my memory. Sonder again: a seesaw across oceans.

The word collectivist comes from the Latin *colligere*, “to choose together.” I used to think that choosing together meant agreeing on political systems, zoning boards, karaoke selections. Now I think it’s the quieter compounding of cumulative mercy. You help the stranger lift the stroller up the subway steps because whoever designed cities didn’t account for the dignity of wheels; she later writes a Yelp review raving about the nutcrackers at that random café her toddler spilled juice on, giving them business enough to avoid eviction; the café owner tutors immigrants in English every Tuesday, one of whom ends up saving his first paycheck to buy a plane ticket to Lisbon, where he watches a woman scatter ash into the freezing sea and sings *toda menina baiana* into the sunset. Everybody wins, or at least nobody loses alone, and no headline will ever document this ecology of generosity.

Sometimes I wonder what would happen if each person carried a holstered QR code linking to a three-minute highlight reel of their year. What fraction would be birthday cake and conferences, and how many would include the early-morning inventory of pills, the voicemail from the landlord, the toddler suddenly yelling “I don’t love you anymore” before nap? Would we still weave through crowds as if collision were a threat instead of communion? At a minimum we might learn to braid our arms differently while waiting in line, offering the body’s semaphore that no part of a person is disposable, not even their baggy left sleeve or mid-calf sock sliding down inside a snow boot.

But short of universal x-ray or code, we practice micro-politics instead. The woman who stocks the lotion sampler and knows I like cedar. The barista with a secret stash of oat milk for lactose-intolerant regulars. The dog groomer who texts photos of poodles smiling like divorce settlements finally finalized. These are small portals someone has tucked into the wall of the world so that strangers don’t have to keep breaking the sheetrock just to find proof of housing. 

I honor my grandfather still, ten years after scattering half of him overseas. Every year on the anniversary I bake the same potato kugel he used to burn the edges on. I omit the onions because I never learned to fix the bitterness. My neighbors know that if they find a foil-wrapped brick on their doorstep it means summer has officially arrived, and the shmaltz steam fogging their windows is a ritual prayer composed of starch and remembrance. They don’t have to know the words. The scent is enough to convert atheists into temporary choirs.

I used to fear that writing about strangers in assembly-line prose would flatten them into props for my catharsis. But we are all each other’s unpaid background extras, it turns out, milling about the set until the moment the camera unpredictably pivots and we become lead actors in someone else’s climactic hospital scene. What saves us from being mere silhouettes is the willingness to concede that someday a stranger’s monologue will hinge on the tenth ofollar bill you handed them in a gas station at dawn, the one that was just enough to pay for diapers enough to last until relief arrived. You will never watch that film. That’s the whole point.

Outside the window, sleet taps against the glass like Morse code sent from whoever died last Thursday and forgot to finish their sentence. Inside, the tea kettle begins its metal cataract hum that sounds indistinguishable from mercy. Whatever molecules my grandfather has become are now suspended in the February wind, riding the same breeze that is today flipping open the collar of the delivery driver’s jacket as he hands me the mislabeled envelope meant for 3C but delivered to 4B. I sign anyway, signifying—what, exactly? That I oblige the planet’s kitchen-table conspiracy to distribute kindness by accident, like breadcrumbs that spell instructions you only read after the birds have already eaten them?

One day, years from now, the teenage boy who held the poodle picture will Yelp-review my kugel under a username born from inside joke. He won’t mention his grandmother’s part in rehab, but he’ll say the casserole tasted like a reprieve. I will never meet him, will never know the elaborately choreographed chain of human pass-along that braided his story into mine. That, too, is sonder. It’s the counterweight you’re holding for somebody else’s constellation, even while you perform ordinary chores of gravity, even as you scatter what can’t be kept or saved, even as you whisper the hundredth name for light into a storm that forgets us the instant it moves on to forgetting another ocean.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
